01 · Problem Statement

A technically capable system
wrapped in friction

The DESE School Finance portal manages state education funding for 500+ Missouri districts. The backend worked. The data was accurate. But the interface had been built in layers over years — each module designed in isolation, with no unified navigation, no dashboard, and no way to understand the state of the system without clicking through every section manually.

The signal: A DESE administrator who had used the system for six years told us she kept a personal spreadsheet to track what was done, what was pending, and what needed attention each month. The spreadsheet was doing the job the dashboard should have been doing.
Three user types, zero role separation
DESE administrators, district LEA staff, and the general public all saw the same interface regardless of role. District staff saw navigation options they couldn't access and data that wasn't scoped to their entity.
Errors surfaced too late
Validation only appeared after submission. Override indicators were visually identical to error states. Approval actions had no cascade warnings — administrators could unknowingly trigger downstream payments.
No calculation status visibility
To find out if ADA or WAM calculations had run, users had to navigate into each section individually. There was no summary view anywhere in the system.
📋
No dashboard existed
Users had no landing page that oriented them to the current state of the system. Every session started with navigation guesswork.
🗂️
Fragmented navigation
20+ unrelated pages listed in build order. No logical grouping. No hierarchy that reflected how users actually work through a monthly cycle.
⚠️
High-stakes, low feedback
Financial workflows involving formula payments, bond disbursements, and ADA data entry had minimal error prevention and no undo capability.
02 · Goals & Objectives

What success
had to look like

🎯
Product Goals
Consolidate 20+ fragmented pages into 7 logical sections
Build a real-time dashboard that surfaces system state at a glance
Role-based scoping — one system, three user contexts
40+ reusable component library for sprint-to-sprint consistency
👥
User Goals
Know the system state without navigating into every section
Enter ADA data with historical context visible during entry
Approve payments with full visibility of cascade effects
District staff see only their entity's data — nothing that doesn't apply to them
📊
Success Metrics
Task completion time reduced ≥40% on core monthly workflows
Data entry errors reduced ≥50%
NPS improvement measurable within 90 days post-launch
Support ticket volume decreasing quarter-over-quarter
03 · Research & Discovery

Learning the domain
before the design

School finance isn't simplified SaaS. ADA, WADA, WAM, LEA, PYC — each term carries precise regulatory meaning. Calculation dependencies are set by Missouri statute. I spent the first weeks learning how the system actually worked before touching a single screen.

Key research constraint: Some workflows couldn't be simplified because the complexity was regulatory, not accidental. My job was to make mandatory complexity navigable — not to remove it.
BA-driven model: Requirements came from Business Analysts based on client needs. Design decisions had to be defendable with domain rationale, not just UX principle.
🗣️
Stakeholder Sessions
Mapped monthly cycles for DESE admins and LEA district staff. Workflows were completely different — yet the system treated them identically.
🗺️
Workflow Mapping
Traced the full monthly cycle: login → calculation status check → ADA entry → payment approval → bond disbursement → reporting. Identified seven distinct task clusters.
❗
Pain Point Audit
Found 11 distinct friction points across the legacy system. The most critical: no calculation status summary, no approval cascade warnings, no role-scoped data views.
🧩
Domain Learning
Learned ADA calculation methodology, WAM override logic, bond payment structures, appropriation frameworks. Designed with domain accuracy, not assumptions.
04 · Information Architecture & User Flow

From 20+ pages
to 7 logical sections

The original navigation was built in the order pages were created — not the order users think. I restructured everything around the monthly workflow sequence a DESE administrator actually follows. The sidebar order now mirrors that sequence.

Why accordion for Yearly Tasks?
Year Rollover, Appropriation, and Maintenance share a workflow context — grouping them reduces cognitive overhead without hiding them.
Why persistent sidebar?
In a high-stakes government application, predictability is a feature. A collapsible sidebar would save pixels but cost users orientation.
05 · Design Process

Components before pages.
System before screens.

I built the component library before touching any page layout. Table headers, status indicators, info/alert boxes, modals, empty states — every reusable element defined first. Pages are assemblies of components, not new designs each time.

This approach meant that sprint-to-sprint, new requirements could be implemented by composing existing components rather than inventing new patterns. Visual consistency was structural, not enforced by review.

Phase 01 · Discovery
Weeks 1–3
Domain learning + stakeholder alignment
Mapped the full monthly cycles for DESE admin and LEA district users. Identified 11 friction points. Learned enough about ADA calculations, WAM overrides, and bond payment structures to make design decisions defensible by domain logic.
Phase 02 · Architecture
Weeks 4–5
Navigation restructure + IA documentation
Restructured 20+ pages into 7 logical sections. Drew.io flow diagrams for each user role (DESE admin, LEA district, public). Sidebar order designed to mirror the monthly workflow sequence — so navigation itself teaches the process.
Phase 03 · System
Weeks 5–7
40+ component library in Figma
Built every reusable component before touching page layouts: table headers (navy + white text, always uppercase), status indicators (colour + icon, never colour alone), button variants (primary, outline, link), form inputs (40px height, label above), info boxes (blue), alert boxes (yellow with left accent), empty states, modals, and conditional disclosure patterns.
Phase 04 · Screen Design
Weeks 7–20+
18 screens across 3 user roles, iterated per sprint
Dashboard, Yearly Tasks, Estimated ADA, Parkway Phase II, Payment Calculations, Bond Payments, LEA Payment Calculations, Reports, Payment Transmittal, and all supporting modals and empty states. Each screen reviewed by stakeholders within its sprint cycle — BA feedback incorporated within days of being raised.
Accessibility Decisions
Status indicators — colour is never enough
Every status pairs colour with a semantic icon. Complete = green + checkmark. Override = amber + triangle. Error = red + X. Screen readers get meaningful labels on all of these. No state is communicated by colour alone — per WCAG 1.4.1.
Keyboard navigation — full tab order
Every interactive element is reachable without a mouse. Focus markers use Royal Blue (#2563EB) — visible and on-brand. Tab index ordered to match reading and workflow order.
Form labels — never inside the field
All form inputs carry labels above the field that remain visible after the field is populated. No placeholder-only patterns. Data tables include explicit ARIA metadata for screen reader compatibility.
Design Documentation Extract
Design System Specification v1.0
06 · High Fidelity UI

Final screens —
annotated for decision rationale

Dashboard — DESE Admin View
dese.mo.gov/school-finance · Dashboard
1
Four KPI cards answer the opening question. Current ADA (3,450), WADA (3,610), Monthly Payment ($152K), Pending Actions (4 — high priority flagged). The Pending Actions card is intentionally designed to draw attention. It's the one number that signals work is needed.
2
ADA/WADA Trends + Monthly Payment Trends. Not decorative — these charts give administrators historical context before they touch data. Pattern recognition happens here. Unusual values stand out before entry, not after submission.
3
Calculation Status surfaces without navigating. Complete, Pending, and Overrides visible on the dashboard. Previously, users had to click into each section individually to find this information. This was the most-requested change in stakeholder sessions.
4
Quick Actions for experienced users. One click to Run Calculations, Enter ADA Data, Generate Report, View Overrides, or Appropriation Report — without touching the sidebar. Efficiency for power users, invisible to new ones.
Payment Calculations — Full View
Payment Calculations — School Finance
1
5 KPI cards standardised to text-xs. Each card has a different data structure — variance figures with directional arrows, multi-line financial summaries, comparison data. Standardising font size created visual rhythm despite the different shapes.
2
Error banner appears contextually only. Not a permanent warning — it fires specifically when calculations are incomplete, with a directive (what's missing, what to do). This specificity is what makes it actionable rather than ignorable.
3
Approve buttons appear conditionally. Only on rows where approval is an available action. Showing them on every row would imply false equivalence and create cognitive noise in a workflow where errors have real financial consequences.
4
Approval cascade modal for Basic Formula State Monies. Clicking Approve triggers a confirmation that names exactly what else will be approved: Host School Payment, Charter School Payment, Charter School Sponsor Payment. Users must understand what they're committing to before releasing funds.
Estimated ADA — 5-Year Trend + Data Entry
Estimated ADA
1
5-year chart before data entry. Historical context isn't a nice-to-have — it's validation infrastructure. ADA errors cascade into payment errors across districts. Seeing prior years before entering current data catches anomalies before submission.
2
District selector locks the entire page. Every number visible is scoped to that district. The most common legacy error was entering data for the wrong district. This pattern eliminates it structurally.
Payment Transmittal — Detail View
Payment Transmittal Detail
1
Revenue breakdown with drill-down links. Line items like "Basic Formula – State Monies" are clickable for deeper context. The table shows Monthly Payment, Minus Bond Direct Deposit, and Net Monthly Payment — the calculation is transparent, not a black box.
LEA Payment Calculations — Empty State
Appropriation Report Modal
Maintenance — System Settings
↑
Empty states — every zero-data context has guidance. The LEA Payment Calculations page only makes sense once a district is selected. Rather than a blank or broken state, it shows: icon + single instructional sentence + support contact. Three elements, no ambiguity. This pattern is applied across every section of the system.
Fund Appropriations & YTD Analysis
Bond Payments — District Ledger
07 · Design System & Consistency

Documented formally.
Not just a Figma file.

The System Interface Design Document v1.0 was delivered alongside the Figma files as a formal client deliverable — covering every colour token, typography scale, spacing rule, and component specification. The intent: the engineering team should never have to guess at a design decision.

Design System Specification v1.0 — Colour Tokens
Colour Token System
Navy Blue
#2C4F7C
Nav, table headers, primary buttons
Royal Blue
#2563EB
Links, focus rings, active states, avatar
Dark Navy
#1E3A5F
Hover state for navy elements
Text Primary
#111827
Headings, primary body text
Text Secondary
#6B7280
Subtitles, helper text, labels
Background
#F9FAFB
Page canvas, table row hover
Yellow Alert
#FEF9C3 / #FBBF24
Accounting notes, system alerts
Blue Info
#EFF6FF / #BFDBFE
Contact resources, info boxes
Typography Scale — System Sans-Serif Stack
ElementScaleWeightColourApplication
Page Titletext-xl · 20pxSemibold 600#111827Primary page headings
Section Labeltext-sm · 14pxSemibold 600#111827Card and section titles
Table Headertext-xs · 12pxSemibold, Uppercase, Tracked#FFFFFF on navyAll data table columns
Table Bodytext-sm · 14pxRegular 400#111827All data rows
Hyperlinkstext-sm · 14pxMedium 500#2563EBInline links, drill-downs
Helper Texttext-xs · 12pxRegular Italic#6B7280Field descriptions, microcopy
Nav Item (active)text-sm · 14pxMedium 500#FFFFFF on #2563EBActive sidebar navigation item
Why system sans-serif? A native font stack — no web font load, no flash of unstyled text, no rendering inconsistency across government-issue desktop monitors. In a data-dense interface, legibility at small sizes outweighs typographic personality.
Typography & Spacing Specification
Page Flow & Navigation Architecture
08 · Challenges & Solutions

The hard decisions
were the interesting ones

⚖️
Complexity that couldn't be removed
School finance calculation rules are set by Missouri statute. The approval chain for Basic Formula payments, the ADA calculation methodology, the distinction between PYC annualised and monthly data — all regulatory, all non-negotiable.
Solution: Make mandatory complexity navigable, not invisible. Progressive disclosure, contextual error messaging, and clear status indicators replace "simplification" where simplification isn't possible.
👥
Three user types, one codebase
Building two completely different interfaces for DESE admin and LEA district staff was the obvious solution. But it would double the maintenance burden and confuse users who move between roles — which happens regularly in government contexts.
Solution: One navigation structure, role-based data scoping. Same components, same patterns — but district staff see only their entity's data. Credential-gated, not interface-gated.
🔄
Live system, mid-cycle redesign
DESE was actively running calculations and processing payments while the redesign was in progress. A full cutover during an active fiscal year wasn't possible. Some structural changes had to be introduced incrementally.
Solution: Preserve familiar structural patterns where users had strong muscle memory; improve information hierarchy and visual clarity within those structures. Migration safety informed design, not just UX quality.
"The approval cascade modal added a step to a single-click action — which I'd typically argue against. But a DESE administrator unknowingly releasing three downstream payments they weren't ready to authorise is a real, costly error. Sometimes friction is the right answer when the stakes justify it."
09 · Impact & Outcomes

Results that came from
real people working differently

The most meaningful outcome wasn't a metric. A DESE administrator who had used the legacy system for six years told us during post-launch review that she no longer keeps her tracking spreadsheet. The system tells her what's done, what's pending, and what needs attention. That was the goal.

40–50%
Faster tasks
Measured across core monthly workflows via user timing studies
60%
Fewer errors
Override frequency tracked before and after redesign
+36
NPS points
32 → 68, across admin and district staff cohorts
45%
Fewer tickets
Support ticket volume down quarter-over-quarter post-launch
10 · Reflection

What I learned.
What's next.

Learned
Domain mastery is a UX skill
I spent significant time learning how ADA calculations work, what a WAM override means in practice, and why the approval chain for Basic Formula payments is structured the way it is. That investment paid off in design decisions I could defend with domain rationale. On complex enterprise work, UX research isn't just user interviews — it's becoming fluent in the domain.
Learned
Systems-first thinking compounds
Building the component library before any page layouts meant that every new sprint requirement was a composition problem, not a design problem. The design system paid dividends with every new screen — and the payoff grew as the project scaled. For complex government platforms, components before pages is non-negotiable.
Improve
Mobile is an unaddressed gap
The system is desktop-first by design and that's appropriate for the primary use case. But district staff increasingly work in hybrid contexts. A read-only mobile view of dashboard data and calculation status would add real value without requiring full mobile parity across all modules — and it's achievable within the existing design system.
Future
Predictive anomaly detection
The system has the architecture to surface warnings when a district's ADA data looks anomalous relative to historical trends — before the user runs calculations to find out. The Recharts infrastructure is in place. The data is available. The next layer of intelligence is a product conversation worth having with DESE stakeholders.
